Hey, what's up? JB is in the house. I am pumped. I'm psyched. I'm ready for an awesome, intensive this week. The topic is obviously going to be prospecting. How do you find the right buyers? And what do you do when you find, let's say, when you get a big group of buyers, how do you sift through that group and make sure that you end up only essentially pitching the right ones? That's the basic way of looking at prospect. In other words, that you're going to get this large group of buyers, right? All right, fair enough. And how do you ask them questions and use that process essentially to say, OK, who is worth me spending the time making a pitch to and who is not? Like, I don't want to waste my time speaking to people that have no money or don't want what I'm selling, right? That's the obvious thing here. But let me just tell you flat out. The higher your closures, you could actually draw a direct relationship with if you spend X amount of time with the prospect, your close-up goes up in lockstep X amount. One of those for instance is vacation sales. If you try to rush through a vacation sale too quickly, just try to clock or get very low close rate. It takes a bit more time of asking questions, really identifying needs getting to the emotional side of the equation. So if you try to rush through that process, you're not going to get anywhere. I'm training guys that was doing tonality training today for the staff, which was ridiculous. All right. Anyway, so you have these four buying archetypes, right? Now, what are they? Well, we know, simply put, right? You have your buyers in heat. Right? That's the first one. Those are the buyers that need one can afford your product and they are feeling urgency. They're feeling some pain. They want to buy now. They've gotten to the point where they say, you know what? I got to fill this need. I got to get a new car right now. I got to get my family covered with insurance. I got to fix the wall, the hole in the wall. I've got to get a new set of windows. I got to get my body work done. I just can't drive around this anymore. Doesn't matter what it is. I've got to get someone to solve my problem. I'm not getting enough leads right now online. I got to hire a digital marketing agency. You get it? Or I don't like my life right. I got to find a second source of income. I thought there was something better for me in life. I've got to do something to make a change. I'm not happy if I just keep going along the same path I'm on. I just don't feel in control. That's a buyer in heat. That's how they feel. When you get on the phone with a buyer in heat, it's really obvious. They're anxious. They're hot for it. Those are the ones that when you do this hybrid type of initial call, where you do the two-call system, but you can transition into a one-call close and I've taught you that in a different lesson. Okay, we'll get to that in scripting for those of you on certification. So you're going to learn exactly that's an amazing secret. I really do that at the highest level. Right? But the point is, is that those are the ones that when you are qualifying them, you could actually close them on the first call. And I say call that could be in person or over the phone. Right? Doesn't matter. Right? Those are your buyers in heat. Right? They're hot. They need to fill the need. They need to fill the need. They need to fill the need right now. Okay. Now, the only drawback with them is that there's not that many of them. You know, it's because like you're on any given day, you have to find someone's in heat that particular moment. Like someone could be a buyer in heat on a Monday and on Wednesday, they're just not anymore, but they still have every intention of buying a product. It's just their own ebb and flow of how they feel. You get it? Like, then see, here's the distinction. The need is the need. There's a need. They have to fill the need. They, they want to fill a need. There's a hole. They want to fill that hole and they're going to do it. They have the money to do it. Right? But being a buyer in heat is like almost, it's a perception. It's a self perception. How do you feel that day? You know, just put yourself, look at it. It's really important to think is what I'm, what I'm driving at here is something very, very, it's like really the keys to the kingdom of being a great prospector. What I'm driving at here is that look at your own life. You wanted something, a product, a good service, right? And you were super hot for it, right? On a Monday and then later that week on Wednesday, you just kind of put it back in your kind of recesses of your mind and you weren't as hot for it on Wednesday. But then a week later, you went out and bought it anyway. You get it? The need, the need was always the need was the need. The need remained, the need remained. But you personally, the way you perceived your life in relation to not having this need filled that changes on a moment to moment basis based on how you feel. So what was I saying? Well, I said, I said, yes, while we're, as the first one, we're not alchemists. We can't turn letting to gold or this into that. Here's what we can do and we can do it really well. We can turn buyers in power into buyers and he that's what you can do. That's the form of alchemy that works as a prospector. So while we're not alchemists, when it comes to transmutating non buyers into buyers, oh my God, yeah, we can with great effect in this, take someone who's right now feeling like they're a buyer in power and actually transform them into a buyer in heat. And you don't have to wait until the looping section. You could do that right from the beginning. This is key. See, again, the new ones here. So when you're prospecting, you're not just looking to sort out the first two groups from the second, from the second two, you're looking within that at the higher level is to take this group, the buyers in power, and you want to transform them into buyers in heat. That's the secret. Because when you do that, when you turn a buyer in power into a buyer in heat, and remember, it happens all the time naturally, all the time. Just remember that. So I'm not asking you to do something. It doesn't naturally occur. Like I said, lead doesn't naturally turn into gold, right? None look, look, look at these don't magically have money fall out of the sky and make them buyers. It doesn't happen. But every day of the week, people who were thinking about buying and felt like they were in power suddenly get triggered, a trigger hits and they become buyers in heat. Every buyer in heat was once a buyer in power. Pretty much. They start off as buyers in power and the urgency builds, the knee grows, they get a sense of all of a sudden, whack, something triggers them. And they say, I have to buy now. What is that trigger? That trigger can come externally. Something happens in their life. Someone makes a comment about them. Someone says something to them. They see someone else and feel something inside. They hear something. They get a letter in the mail. Oops. You know, they get a letter in the mail from the IRS. And suddenly they're like, oh my God, I'm a buyer in heat. One of those tax, you know, companies that's helped me settle IRS taxes. You suddenly become a buyer in heat. Now you owe the money before that. But the day you get the letter from the IRS, the levees two weeks away, you suddenly become a buyer in heat. We naturally become buyers in heat. And then what happens is you'll still be a buyer. What happens? Let's say all of a sudden you push it back because it's just too much to worry about and become a buyer in power. You stop worrying about stuff. You go back to being a buyer in power. There are things that you do on the call or in person that ratchet up someone's pain threshold. Now, in a case like for instance of the John Maxwell group, how do you ethically and elegantly do that? Well, guess what? The people that are coming to you because they want more in life, they want something more. They've had maybe a little bit of success. Maybe they're looking for now a second income or some more purpose or meaning have a purpose driven life. Well, how would you accomplish that? Well, I'm helping you guys write your script, right? So you can know exactly how the point is very simple. It would start not when you make your presentation and you start trying to solve their problem. So what happens when you do that is you actually transform, you take someone who's considering something and you make them really want it now in the moment. So this is the first distinction. I really know above the basics of prospecting, all right, is knowing how to essentially elegantly, meaning without pissing them off, elegantly taking a buyer in power and building that urgency up so they become a buyer in hate. Crucial. Now, let's move on to the next nuance of straight line prospecting. I want you to be thinking about it all the time. When you ask someone questions, right? You're clearly looking to you're clearly looking to identify needs, right? What are their primary needs? What are their secondary needs, their tertiary needs? What are their values, their beliefs, their pain points, right? Those are the basic stuff you're looking to do, right? But as you're doing that, there's a certain way that you sound a certain way that a certain way that you ask questions, a certain order in which you ask those questions that make you sound extremely intelligent, that make you sound with no uncertainty, because you are an expert in your field. You're someone that's going to help them solve their problem. That is the second part of prospecting that you must. It's like a must. OK, it's not just about separating out the good ones from the bad ones, right? That's the simple stuff. All right. Ask questions. You find who's got money who doesn't need you. But very basic. The this next level up is that while you're talking and while you're hunting, while you're finishing some of their sentences, asking them pointed questions, following a logical train of thought, it starts to become incredibly apparent to this person that you are very much an expert in your field and that you are a person that can help them solve their problems and get rid of their pain and make them feel more back in control of their life. Because at the end of the day, that's what this is about. Making someone feel like they're back in control again in their life. That's what a great salesperson does. You put someone back in control. You put them back in the driver's seat. So now when I actually have my have these rules up in order, it's when I go through these rules and a very set of guidelines and rules for how you go about engaging in an effective, straight line prospecting session. Well, guess what? At the heart of it all is the idea that if you follow these rules, you set yourself up to be perceived exactly the right way as an expert in your field. In other words, in the first four seconds, you establish this perception. That's what those first four seconds are about. And then those 15, 30 seconds that follow as you explain the reason why you're calling who your company is, what you have to offer. And then you see if they're interested or not in even letting you qualify them. Right. That's that little transitional phrase, right? The positioning that you initially got, expert or not, is not set in stone. It's fluid. It's fragile. And this is the point in the sale of people. I said they die from a death of a thousand cuts. You know, it's not any one thing that you do that ultimately destroys you. It's like you don't sound right. So for example, one of the rules here is I say you always want to use a script. The prospecting. Now, if you're in person, obviously you're not like, well, Mr. Jones, that's not what I mean. What I'm saying is you want to have strategically prepared yourself. You want to essentially make sure that before you enter an intelligence gathering session, you've thought about, okay, what the way this is you think what is this process going to look like? Imagine it going from start to finish. If I say, if I ask X, how many different types of Y's can he respond? He could say this, you know, it's only going to be a few. When you ask someone a question, there's only a number of ways they can respond. And then based on all those responses, where's my flow? Chug, what am I? What are my possible follow up questions? This is something that you must script out beforehand. You can't leave it up to chance guys. It's the biggest mistake that sales people make in this part of the sale. They feel like, well, you know, I don't know what the guy's going to say, or the girl's going to say, how can I script it all you can? There's only so many things that people say. There's only so many responses to any question and you can have them all mapped out in advance and based on how they respond, that dictates where you go next. It could all be scripted out and whether you're in person or on the phone, doesn't matter if it's on the phone. Well, that's great. You got your script right in front of you. If your questions is anchors and I'll tell you, I never, when I'm on a telephone call selling, I always want to have my script in front of me just as an anchor. Always, always, always. That's a must. If I'm selling out in the field, yeah, I'm not going to have it in front of me. But there are, by the many ways I can tip that balance in my favor. For example, if I'm selling a product like mortgages or insurance or something that requires me or allows me to have a clipboard or a panel, like, you know, my own folder where I'm taking notes, I could be opening it up or using a questionnaire thing. Like sometimes you have legal things that are legally required to be asked, right? You use that as your script. You have your questions filtered in there. So even when you're in person, there are ways to actually create a little like kind of crib notes for you so you know exactly where you're going next. And when you're on the phone, you just have it right in front of you. All right, I'm just taking one sip here. I'm still recording from yoga, by the way. I also knew I also have some grapes. Yeah, I mean, I need some sugar. I also knew I also have some grapes. Yeah, I mean, I need a sugar shot. Yeah. Oh, well, boom. Yeah, so I got to chew your. Anyway, so that's like a must. It's a rule. Like why on earth would you leave this up the chance? Please. Just like it's like it's like the gift you give yourself to prepare your questions in advance. Now, once you do that, well, guess what? It opens up a world of possibility, a world of the possibility. For instance, we know there are certain like cornerstone rules here. These sort of make or break things that I want you to think about right now. Number one, the biggest faux pas here, like the death now of all prospecting sessions is asking invasive questions too early. You understand? And asking. And I'm going to ask an invasive questions too early. What is an invasive question? And this is the issue where people scrub. See what you might think is an invasive question might actually not be an invasive question. The definition of an invasive question that you probably think is you're probably thinking invasive question is a question that requires my prospect to reveal some personal information. That's not an invasive question. The operative term here, well, before I even answer, let's watch. So what rate are you paying on your, what's your current interest rate on your mortgage? Is that an invasive question? What's my interest rate? Actually, it's not an invasive question. You know why? Because the answer to that question would not reveal anything that really matters. That is personal to the prospect. It's a specific question. But it's not an invasive question. When you say what rate you're paying on your mortgage? Well, it's really what you're saying is, is how is your timing when you took out your loan? It doesn't say that someone's, you know, uh, stuff smart or stupid. Now if it's 15% and you say, well, you're a credit criminal, right? Different issue. But I'm talking about that the question itself is not invasive. Invasement is how much do you owe on your home right now? That's more invasive. But what is your payment? What's your monthly payment? Now, if you went to someone and I was, you know, again, I sold, you know, I sold mortgages door to door. Best that ever, right? And when you would go and knock on someone's home or in their business, you say, hey, what rate are you paying? Right? Anyone would say, hey, you know, I'd say, my name is Jordan from Mortgage Broken Area. What rate are you paying on your mortgage right now? You're paying over 8%. I could save you a ton of money. What rate you're paying? They'd say, I'm paying nine. Oh my God. I could definitely save you a ton of money. And that opened up my book and say, great. So what area do you live in? That's my first question. What area do you live in? So I'm living up in Westchester County. I've been in the Poughkeepsie. Oh, great. Cool. And how are we living there for? There's about 60 years. Oh, cool. And I'm in single family house or a part of a single family. Okay. Oh, great. Great. And I'm living in the Poughkeepsie. No single family. I'm okay. Oh, great. Great. And by the way, in that area, was that a ranch house? I asked three or four questions. Right. And okay. Awesome. And by and what are you currently on the mortgage right now? Just a ballpark. The fourth question. Say, ah, 250. In other words, what would happen is, is I would net you can ask someone what they owe question five or four. If you open up your clipboard, say, oh, what do you want on the mortgage right now? Like screw you. I promise you this. Let's think about that. I asked someone, oh, how much do you owe on your mortgage right now? That's a pretty personal question. What do you owe to the bank on your home? Right. You never would ask that question first or second. It comes fifth. Maybe sixth. I would never say this on what are you currently paying? What's your monthly payment? Right now. That's more invasive. Okay. So what I would say is, oh, you know, you live on nice area. Oh, well, homes there go for what, like $700,000. Cause once you know, if you know your area, you'll know basically what homes go for. Oh, nice area. What's about, you know, beautiful area was homes about a million bucks. They're like, great. Wow. Great. How long you're living there for? Anyone would answer that question. How long you're living there for? How long you're working at that job for? Oh, you got any kids? Oh, a nice school district. Those are basic questions anyone would answer. And I say, oh, what do you want in the home? I just a ballpark to 50 and they'll answer you every single time. You know, I maybe have like one or two people a month at a 500 people that would say, ah, that's kind of personal. And I say, oh, listen, no, because I just want to get a sense here. So what do you think the home, what do you think your home is worth? Just a ballpark? Oh, maybe 750. Okay. Great. And what do you think, you know, and so what do you, you know, it's a ballpark. Give me an estimate. Oh, 200. The last year in the second time. But the point is, if you try to ask it first, you're going to get destroyed. So please, you need to understand what the difference between a specific question is and an invasive question. A specific question is something that is specific to your industry. It's information that you need to have to find out if the product fits for them, if they can afford it, if it makes sense to do the deal, right? But specific doesn't mean invasive. Invasive means that they're revealing information that is personal to them. That might be something they don't want published, you know, out in the newspaper or like on the internet or on, you know, whatever, on social media. That's invasive. So you gotta be very, very careful because if you hit someone with an invasive question upfront, it breaks rapport so dramatically that all the good will that you've built up is thrown out the window just like that. It happens that fast. That's how destructive an invasive question can be. But you have to ask them. You gotta ask them. Okay. So very often what you'll do is you'll, you'll pepper in your invasive questions. But towards the end, you start off with these more big picture questions. Questions that anyone would answer. Now, in their certification, I gave you a list of some big picture questions that we use with great effect. I'll tell you three of them right now that we ask in sequence, right? Those magic three, you know, you know, what do you like about your current source? What do you like about the current supplier? What do you like about the current broker you're dealing with? What do you like about the current product? It's because many times, remember, this doesn't apply to every industry, but it applies to many. Just follow me through this sequence of three questions. Say to someone, so tell me, you know, what do you like about the current product? What do you like about it? What's good about it? You're looking to typically replace what someone already has. Could be a, you know, a digital market. They have another, another person. What do you like about the service? What do they do well? Uh-huh. Oh, okay. Got it. Um-hmm. Yep. Of course. Right. Makes sense. Right. And then what don't you like? What don't they do so? What are the things that really deficient? What are the things that really drive you crazy? Tell me about that. You asked the positive first and then the negative second. See, what happens is you, what you don't want to do is you do not want to ask the negative first. So tell me, what don't you like about what you're using? That's too, he's too in your face, you're sharp shooting them. That is a way to break rapport. So in other words, if you say, so what don't you like about your current product? It's a, because I want to know what it's like, they can tell you that mine is better so I can replace it and make money in my commission. That's like such a direct in their face. A front. What do you not like about the product you currently have? You can't ask that first in a vacuum because it's so obvious it on the nose and hence it breaks rapport. So instead, what we do is we flip it. We say, so what do you like about the product you're currently using? There's going to be some features and benefits they like. Uh huh. Uh huh. Yep. Of course. Right. And what don't you now? The next question it begs to be asked is, is what? What don't you like? See now it starts to make sense. And then the third question in sequences and body, if you can design this for yourself, what would it look like? What were the features would have? What benefits, you know, what would it look like? And obviously you just tailor that to your specific industry, whatever you're selling. And then they'll literally hand you the keys to the kingdom right there. They'll say, oh, I like this is important to me. That's important to me. And now of course, guess what? When you make your presentation, a.k.a. or for your solution. Hello, what are you doing? You're going to focus on the things that are deficient to them that they make sure you hit those. Obviously, all things they like, oh, yeah, we have this, we have that. And we also have now the kid this. And then by the way, you're going to make sure it's tailored exactly the way they want. They give you a roadmap to close them. Do you understand? It's so simple yet so few sales people do that. They rush through intelligence gathering, thinking only of this one thing. I got to separate out the buyers from the non-buyers. And by the way, a lot of people don't even do that. They just said, which one? Say, I want to see if the guys got money. We don't just rush through it. They don't dig into the deeper levels of this, which are you can elevate buyers in power easily. Turn them into buyers of heat. And then also as you're doing that, you can also have them design what that perfect solution for them would look like. Why would you not want to do that? I always do that. I just, you know, I always do that. When I was in the mall, you know, what kind of malls you're in now? What movie that one in the past? What did you like? What did you like about them? Oh, there were this, that there were there. What didn't you like on it? What kind of perfect malls would that look like? How many years would it be? It would be flexible, it would be fixed with a prepaid number. You want to pull money out of your house, get equity out of your house, pay for credit cards. Tell me. Well, I'd like what I really love to do is I'd love to be able to roll up all my credit cards. I'd love to get a rate that's like, you know, I don't mind. I'm probably going to sell in five years. I want it fixed for the first five years. I get a lower rate right now. I could pay interest only. I don't really want to pay you. You don't pay any. They'll tell you what they want. Hmm. Well, let me say this based on what you just said. We have to here's the perfect fit. Let me tell you what we got. Here's the program. Hello. Why wouldn't you do that? Why would you take the time to find out what someone exactly wants? It's not just about that. Now, you obviously this takes strategy and tactics to get this out of people. You got to ask this. So I have all these different lessons that we go through, right? In sort of case we dig into the reason I dig so deeply into all these lessons is because this is like that death by a thousand cut syndrome, where if you do one thing wrong and each guy's a nick, a nick, another nick, you get nick, nick, nick, you slowly bleeding out, right? And then before you know it, you bled out and it's over. OK, you get it. Now, other things, very simple, right? About, you know, always following a logical path. What does that really mean? Well, quite simply, if you don't do that, you sound like a moron. And what are morons? Morons equal what? Novices, like moron equal novice, expert, equal spot person. When you don't follow a logical path, that's when you ping pong, where like you're just one question to you. Oh, so how long are you living in the area for? They say, oh, five years. Oh, great. And what did you pay for your home? And when your kids go, what? You like that, you know, before you know it, like this person is an idiot. The best example I give this is a doctor. And you see a really well trained doctor with a good bedside bed. And I cares, right? What do they do? They take you to a very deliberate. Intelligence gathering session where they go from big picture to small picture. They follow a chronological order. Don't say uses anything that I've left out, anything else you want to tell me. They always ask you that, a good doctor. And then only then after that, when they know everything they need to know, they'll say, well, based on what you said to me, this is what I recommend. And that could be a medication, surgery or do nothing at all, physical therapy. So that happens, though, after a logically progressed intelligence gathering session. Just imagine how you'd feel. I know how I felt because it happened once with a doctor. Just like you walk in like, oh, you need surgery. Screw you. I'm more than just a number. I'm more than an x-ray. Take the time to find out what your clients need. Take time to find out what's causing their pain and make sure that when you're asking those pain based questions that amplify pain, that you ask them in the right tonality. That's why it's one of the rules of prospects. We go through the whole lesson on that, right? Asking questions in the right tonality. So what is it that's really keeping you up at night? What's that one way to get your attention? When do you think it's going to happen if you don't do anything? Where do you see yourself in a couple of years from now? What's going to happen if you don't do anything about that? What's the impact it's going to have on your wife, on your family? People you love and care about? What's going to happen if you ask it the other way? So what's going to happen if you don't take action? How strew will you be? They'll hate you. You break for poor. So what's the impact? What's the impact on your family? What's the impact on you? They'll hate you. You break for poor. That's a gross exaggeration, obviously, but the point is the way you ask your questions is crucial. When I see people who are in a slump selling, I always notice two things. Ready with the arm? Number one, they're rushing through a qualification. They rush through the intelligence gathering phase almost all the time and secondly, they pitch no matter what the people say. Doesn't matter what they hear. It doesn't matter. Oh, they're gonna shove them all into this round hole, but how squared that peg might be. That's what you see when people are in slumps. So please and again in this lesson, we did a really big interactivity about your sales funnel with designing your sales funnel, right? And by the way, how great was the gatekeeper lesson, right? That was a module to itself, right? So you guys who are on, if you're not, go on certification, please. Give yourself a gift, right? So for those of you who go, please, if you haven't been through it yet, right? Please go through those lessons on gatekeepers, on sales funnel. I'm not closing the secretary to buy a product. You close the boss, the owner. You gotta get through the screen. And you have to understand the different steps in your sales funnel. Where does marketing leave off and where does sales begin? One of the questions that was asked on the platform today was, it was like, obviously the person is not on certification. None. You don't get, it's not sales, it's marketing. You don't, sales is not getting more leads. That's telemarketing. Now there's a sales process that allows you to be an effective telemarketer. But sales begins, the sales side of it begins, the moment someone opens up their mouth and says, yeah, I might be interested in what do you got? So remember, I always tell this, I tell a great story about when I was 10 years or eight years old, actually, I'm my first paper out. And I was struggling to grow my paper out because I thought intuitively as a kid that if I had made a list of all the doors that didn't have a newspaper in front, I mean, you could tell who was getting papers delivered and who wasn't, right? If someone dropped a paper at someone's doorstep, well, they were a customer or someone else. But those people that did not have a paper at the door front, those were people who need to get a paper. So instinctively, intuitively, I was like, wow, I made a list of all those doors that I never saw another newspaper at as I went around to live my own paper, right? And then when I actually started knocking on those doors, guess what? Zero success. I think like two or three weeks later, I hadn't opened up a single new customer, not one. And when I knocked on these doors, you know what happened 90% of the time? No one answered. And 10% of the time when they did, guess what? They weren't interested. And it drove me crazy. I said, I don't understand. Any of those virgin territories didn't make sense to me, right? And my friend's dad, it was a mock and he got clarified. I said, Jordan, profoundly, he said, there's two types of people in the world. There's those that want to get a paper delivered and those who don't. He goes, right now you're knocking on all those of those who don't. I was like, really? He goes, yeah, remember this. Either people want to read a paper at home or don't. They might get it. I said, but they'll read more than one paper. Because why don't you try this? Why don't you knock on all the doors that deliver, that get the New York Times? You deliver the post. They'll read two papers. People who read papers will read more than one paper. People who go and buy dresses and stores go and shop in more than one shop. People who go online and buy things online. They weeded out the non-buyers from the buyers. All I had to was find out who the buyers were. They were buying other papers. And give them a shot to buy another one. Elegantly logical. All right. You want to do a couple more questions? Sure. Should we do them? This one's from on. Okay. Let's just just a couple of speed ones because there's a couple more left. Hey JB, I'm into FMCG sector selling products such as flour, cleaner, toiletries, etc. My prospect owns a chain of departmental stores across the country selling items similar to mine. Can you please help me with a few of the qualifying questions? I'm selling them. And what was that? Ask what? Fast moving commodity goods. Okay. And he sells them to someone who's... I assume. So are you saying you're a wholes... You're selling to someone who's then retail? That what you're saying? Yeah. He's a wholesaler selling them. He's selling to a retailer. Okay. Yeah. It seems like it. And what's the question? And he's asking if you could help him with some few qualifying questions. All right. Well, so my friend, let me ask you this. Okay. And that was what I was saying, I think to the first person who said like, what would be some good questions to ask someone in the propane? Work backwards. What... I think one thing, and I really discussed this in one of the later lessons on prospecting, you know, prospecting is not magic. Scientific. Always ask yourself this question. What do I need to know to make an informed decision if this guy's a buyer or not? Ask yourself that. Does he need the product? In your case, it seems they probably do, right? They're selling what you're selling. All right. Are they happy with their current supplier? What are they currently paying for the goods they say? Can you beat the price? Is there a problem with lead times? Is there a problem with how long the credit they get? Find out what are the pain points with the people who you're looking to replace? Are you looking to sell them things they're not currently selling? That's one set of questions. Are you replacing items that they are selling or is it new products? You're looking to get shelfies to give you a shot. It sounds like you're saying commodity, which by its very definition means you're selling common goods that are basically commodity items. So they come down to price, speed of delivery, credit terms, customer service, when something goes wrong. The biggest things, it always turns around. So if I'm a store owner, I own department stores, I buy for them. What are the things that are important to me? What do I need? I need to have a reliable source. I want to make sure they have inventory stock. I can get something from the pinch. I want to make sure I'm getting the right price from them. I'm getting the qualities always up the par. Those are the things that are important. Work backwards from there. Ask questions that will ascertain, illuminate whether or not this is a person that you could potentially help. That's how you do it. Three more? Yeah. Michael says, JB, does the straight line work well for the real estate cold calling or warm leads? Oh my God. It's amazing for that. Perfect for it. Perfect for that. I've spoken at so many real estate conventions. I've trained so many people who sell build homes, sell homes. And you name it.
